options.why: The vertical scale already goes up in equal steps of $2$.
The horizontal axis is already labelled "Colour".
Each bar is already named (Red, Blue, White, Silver, Black).

questions.solution_text:
The axes and scale are present, but nothing states what the graph is about.
A title is missing.

options.text: A label on the vertical axis

options.why: The graph already has the title across the top.
The vertical axis already increases in equal steps of $2$.
A source is optional here; the reader still cannot tell what the vertical numbers count.

questions.solution_text:
The vertical axis shows numbers but not what they measure.
A vertical axis label is missing.

questions.question_text:
A frequency axis on a histogram is numbered $0, 2, 4, 6, 10, 12$.
Which graph convention does this break?

options.text: The scale must go up in equal steps

options.why: The scale does start at $0$; the fault is the uneven jump from $6$ to $10$.

options.text: Frequency must be on the horizontal axis
options.why: Frequency belongs on the vertical axis of a histogram, so this is not the fault.

options.text: Columns must have gaps between them
options.why: Histogram columns touch by design; this is about the axis scale, not the bars.

questions.solution_text:
The steps are $2, 2, 2, 4, 2$, so the jump from $6$ to $10$ is uneven.
A scale must increase in equal steps.

questions.question_text:
A column graph compares two teams using green and orange bars, but nothing tells the reader what each colour represents.
Which convention is missing?

options.why: A title names the whole graph; it would not say which colour is which team.
The scale is unrelated to explaining what the two colours mean.
A source says where the data came from, not what each colour represents.

questions.solution_text:
Two colours stand for two teams, so the reader must be told which colour is which.
A key (legend) is missing.
